question:
6. \( S_{1}=\ln a \) and \( S_{n}=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \ln \left(a-S_{i}\right), n>1 \).

Show that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} S_{n}=a-1
\]
solution:
Solution. The given recursion can be written
\[
S_{n+1}=S_{n}+\ln \left(a-S_{n}\right)
\]

The polygonal representation of this recursion is shown in the figure [see p. 223]. It is clear that, with any choice of \( S_{1}<a \), we have
\[
S_{2} \leq S_{3} \leq S_{4} \leq \cdots \leq a-1
\]
and that the sequence converges to \( a-1 \).
To prove this analytically, we let
\[
f(x)=x+\ln (a-x)
\]
for \( x<a \). Then \( f^{\prime}(x)=1-1 /(a-x) \), which is positive for \( x<a-1 \) and negative for \( x>a-1 \). Since \( f(a-1)=a-1 \), it follows that \( f(x) \leq a-1 \) for all \( x<a \). Also, if \( x \leq a-1 \), then \( \ln (a-x) \geq 0 \), so \( f(x) \geq x \). Then (1) follows immediately, so the sequence \( \left\{S_{n}\right\} \) has a limit, say \( T \). Clearly, \( T \leq a-1 \), so \( T \) is a point of continuity for \( f \). Hence
\[
f(T)=f\left(\lim S_{n}\right)=\lim f\left(S_{n}\right)=\lim S_{n+1}=T
\]

This gives \( \ln (a-T)=0 \), and therefore \( T=a-1 \).
We have proved \( \lim S_{n}=a-1 \), as required.

descriptive_long.question:
6. \( firstsum = \ln constant \) and \( nthsum = \sum_{i=1}^{iterindex-1} \ln \left( constant - S_{i} \right),\; iterindex>1 \).

Show that
\[
\lim _{iterindex \rightarrow \infty} nthsum = constant-1
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The given recursion can be written
\[
nextsum = nthsum + \ln \left( constant - nthsum \right)
\]

The polygonal representation of this recursion is shown in the figure [see p. 223]. It is clear that, with any choice of \( firstsum < constant \), we have
\[
secondsum \leq thirdsum \leq fourthsum \leq \cdots \leq constant-1
\]
and that the sequence converges to \( constant-1 \).
To prove this analytically, we let
\[
function(variable)=variable+\ln (constant-variable)
\]
for \( variable < constant \). Then \( function^{\prime}(variable)=1-1 /(constant-variable) \), which is positive for \( variable < constant-1 \) and negative for \( variable > constant-1 \). Since \( function(constant-1)=constant-1 \), it follows that \( function(variable) \leq constant-1 \) for all \( variable < constant \). Also, if \( variable \leq constant-1 \), then \( \ln (constant-variable) \geq 0 \), so \( function(variable) \geq variable \). Then (1) follows immediately, so the sequence \( \left\{ nthsum \right\} \) has a limit, say \( limitvalue \). Clearly, \( limitvalue \leq constant-1 \), so \( limitvalue \) is a point of continuity for \( function \). Hence
\[
function(limitvalue)=function\left(\lim nthsum\right)=\lim function\left(nthsum\right)=\lim nextsum=limitvalue
\]

This gives \( \ln (constant-limitvalue)=0 \), and therefore \( limitvalue = constant-1 \).
We have proved \( \lim nthsum = constant-1 \), as required.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. \( blueprint=\ln waterfall \) and \( shoreline=\sum_{lanterns=1}^{sailboat-1} \ln \left(waterfall-S_{lanterns}\right),\ sailboat>1 \).

Show that
\[
\lim _{sailboat \rightarrow \infty} shoreline=waterfall-1
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The given recursion can be written
\[
limestone=shoreline+\ln \left(waterfall-shoreline\right)
\]

The polygonal representation of this recursion is shown in the figure [see p. 223]. It is clear that, with any choice of \( blueprint<waterfall \), we have
\[
daydream \leq frostbite \leq blackbird \leq \cdots \leq waterfall-1
\]
and that the sequence converges to \( waterfall-1 \).
To prove this analytically, we let
\[
windchime(hedgehog)=hedgehog+\ln (waterfall-hedgehog)
\]
for \( hedgehog<waterfall \). Then \( windchime^{\prime}(hedgehog)=1-1 /(waterfall-hedgehog) \), which is positive for \( hedgehog<waterfall-1 \) and negative for \( hedgehog>waterfall-1 \). Since \( windchime(waterfall-1)=waterfall-1 \), it follows that \( windchime(hedgehog) \leq waterfall-1 \) for all \( hedgehog<waterfall \). Also, if \( hedgehog \leq waterfall-1 \), then \( \ln (waterfall-hedgehog) \geq 0 \), so \( windchime(hedgehog) \geq hedgehog \). Then (1) follows immediately, so the sequence \( \left\{shoreline\right\} \) has a limit, say \( campfire \). Clearly, \( campfire \leq waterfall-1 \), so \( campfire \) is a point of continuity for \( windchime \). Hence
\[
windchime(campfire)=windchime\left(\lim shoreline\right)=\lim windchime\left(shoreline\right)=\lim limestone=campfire
\]

This gives \( \ln (waterfall-campfire)=0 \), and therefore \( campfire=waterfall-1 \).
We have proved \( \lim shoreline=waterfall-1 \), as required.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. \( differenceone=\ln variable \) and \( differencegeneral=\sum_{outlander=1}^{specific-1} \ln \left(variable-S_{outlander}\right), specific>1 \).

Show that
\[
\lim _{specific \rightarrow \infty} differencegeneral=variable-1
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The given recursion can be written
\[
differenceplusone=differencegeneral+\ln \left(variable-differencegeneral\right)
\]

The polygonal representation of this recursion is shown in the figure [see p. 223]. It is clear that, with any choice of \( differenceone<variable \), we have
\[
differencetwo \leq differencethree \leq differencefour \leq \cdots \leq variable-1
\]
and that the sequence converges to \( variable-1 \).
To prove this analytically, we let
\[
nonfunction(constant)=constant+\ln (variable-constant)
\]
for \( constant<variable \). Then \( nonfunction^{\prime}(constant)=1-1 /(variable-constant) \), which is positive for \( constant<variable-1 \) and negative for \( constant>variable-1 \). Since \( nonfunction(variable-1)=variable-1 \), it follows that \( nonfunction(constant) \leq variable-1 \) for all \( constant<variable \). Also, if \( constant \leq variable-1 \), then \( \ln (variable-constant) \geq 0 \), so \( nonfunction(constant) \geq constant \). Then (1) follows immediately, so the sequence \( \left\{differencegeneral\right\} \) has a limit, say \( startpoint \). Clearly, \( startpoint \leq variable-1 \), so \( startpoint \) is a point of continuity for \( nonfunction \). Hence
\[
nonfunction(startpoint)=nonfunction\left(\lim differencegeneral\right)=\lim nonfunction\left(differencegeneral\right)=\lim differenceplusone=startpoint
\]

This gives \( \ln (variable-startpoint)=0 \), and therefore \( startpoint=variable-1 \).
We have proved \( \lim differencegeneral=variable-1 \), as required.

garbled_string.question:
6. \( zqmnplsk=\ln tpvzhrma \) and \( xvytrmjq=\sum_{vfdlrzwo=1}^{jzmqktua-1} \ln \left(tpvzhrma-S_{vfdlrzwo}\right), jzmqktua>1 \).

Show that
\[
\lim _{jzmqktua \rightarrow \infty} xvytrmjq=tpvzhrma-1
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The given recursion can be written
\[
rhqtsvyd=xvytrmjq+\ln \left(tpvzhrma-xvytrmjq\right)
\]

The polygonal representation of this recursion is shown in the figure [see p. 223]. It is clear that, with any choice of \( zqmnplsk<tpvzhrma \), we have
\[
ljkherop \leq wndqslvo \leq gpmzkehu \leq \cdots \leq tpvzhrma-1
\]
and that the sequence converges to \( tpvzhrma-1 \).
To prove this analytically, we let
\[
snrqmgav(hzyplmvn)=hzyplmvn+\ln (tpvzhrma-hzyplmvn)
\]
for \( hzyplmvn<tpvzhrma \). Then \( snrqmgav^{\prime}(hzyplmvn)=1-1 /(tpvzhrma-hzyplmvn) \), which is positive for \( hzyplmvn<tpvzhrma-1 \) and negative for \( hzyplmvn>tpvzhrma-1 \). Since \( snrqmgav(tpvzhrma-1)=tpvzhrma-1 \), it follows that \( snrqmgav(hzyplmvn) \leq tpvzhrma-1 \) for all \( hzyplmvn<tpvzhrma \). Also, if \( hzyplmvn \leq tpvzhrma-1 \), then \( \ln (tpvzhrma-hzyplmvn) \geq 0 \), so \( snrqmgav(hzyplmvn) \geq hzyplmvn \). Then (1) follows immediately, so the sequence \( \left\{xvytrmjq\right\} \) has a limit, say \( cklwodij \). Clearly, \( cklwodij \leq tpvzhrma-1 \), so \( cklwodij \) is a point of continuity for \( snrqmgav \). Hence
\[
snrqmgav(cklwodij)=snrqmgav\left(\lim xvytrmjq\right)=\lim snrqmgav\left(xvytrmjq\right)=\lim rhqtsvyd=cklwodij
\]

This gives \( \ln (tpvzhrma-cklwodij)=0 \), and therefore \( cklwodij=tpvzhrma-1 \).
We have proved \( \lim xvytrmjq=tpvzhrma-1 \), as required.

kernel_variant.question:
Let c>d>0 with c-d>2.  Define  
 S_1 = log_2(c/d), and for n \geq  1  
  S_{n+1}= S_n + (ln 2)\cdot [log_2(c-S_n) - log_2(d+S_n)].  
Prove that the sequence (S_n) converges and determine  
 lim_{n\to \infty } S_n.
kernel_variant.solution:
1. Fixed-point form.  
Since (ln 2)\cdot log_2x = ln x, write

 S_{n+1}=f(S_n), f(x)=x+ln[(c-x)/(d+x)], -d<x<c.

2. Shape of f.  
Differentiating gives f'(x)=1-1/(c-x)-1/(d+x).  
The equation f'(x)=0 yields the unique critical point

 x_0=(c-d)/2.

Because f'>0 for x<x_0 and f'<0 for x>x_0, f rises until x_0 and then falls, so

 f(x)\leq f(x_0)=x_0.

3. Bounding the orbit.  
Since c-d>2 we have c/d\geq 2, hence S_1=log_2(c/d)\leq 1\leq x_0.  
Assume S_n\leq x_0.  Then (c-S_n)/(d+S_n)\geq 1, so ln[(c-S_n)/(d+S_n)]\geq 0 and f(S_n)\geq S_n; monotonicity on (-\infty ,x_0] also gives f(S_n)\leq x_0.  Thus

 S_n \uparrow   and  S_n\leq x_0 for every n.

Therefore (S_n) is increasing and bounded, hence convergent; write T:=lim S_n.

4. Identification of the limit.  
By continuity of f,

 T=f(T) \Rightarrow  ln[(c-T)/(d+T)]=0 \Rightarrow  c-T=d+T \Rightarrow  T=(c-d)/2.

5. Result.  
Consequently lim_{n\to \infty } S_n = (c-d)/2.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
